slot1.heading:
Before you start,
check the results first
slot1.sub: Pick a sample session and see the session note draft Modalia AI prepared.

step1.label: Step 1 / 3 — Choose a sample case
step1.lead: First, choose a sample case to explore
step1.sub: These are example cases for experiencing Modalia AI — not real client data.

01.title:
20s professional,
self-blame
01.situ: A first-session case of a client who reports worry about work mistakes and repetitive pre-sleep rumination.

step2.label: Step 2 / 3 — Secure processing
step2.sub: Sample data, but the processing follows the same steps as a real session.

step3.label: Step 3 / 3 — Session note results
step3.lead: Here's how your session note draft comes out

rows.label: Counseling Topic
rows.value: Recurring anticipatory anxiety and self-blame in performance evaluation situations, extending to sleep disruption and reduced daily functioning.

rows.label: Session Goals
rows.value: Identify the specific cycle of automatic thoughts triggered in work situations, and establish a starting point for cognitive defusion work to reduce pre-sleep rumination.

rows.label: Session Summary
rows.value: Three core automatic thoughts — 'I messed up / Everyone noticed / I've lost their trust' — repeat in a four-stage cycle: post-meeting → self-blame → rumination → sleep disruption. Toward the end of the session, a brief 'one thing I did well today' self-affirmation exercise was attempted…

rows.label: Main Intervention Direction
rows.value: Cognitive restructuring questions to separate facts from interpretations. Progressively applying distancing work from automatic thoughts, and building metacognitive awareness of how self-blame maintains anxiety.

rows.label: Next Session Plan
rows.value: Select one recent moment the client felt they had made a mistake, examine the thoughts, physical reactions, and subsequent behaviors, and conduct a perspective-broadening exercise by comparing with a colleague's viewpoint.

02.name: Couples Conflict — Communication breakdown, co-parenting conflict

rows.label: Counseling Topic
rows.value: Couples conflict driven by recurring communication breakdown and differing parenting approaches

rows.label: Session Goals
rows.value: Identify recurring interaction patterns during conflict and examine each partner's emotional responses separately.

rows.label: Session Summary
rows.value: Conflict frequency around parenting has increased over the past six months, with communication becoming entrenched in a blame–defensiveness–avoidance cycle.

rows.label: Points for Counselor Review
rows.value: Beyond the surface content of conflict, recurring communication styles and emotional distance need to be examined together in the next session.

rows.label: Next Session Plan
rows.value: Reconstruct one recent conflict scene in detail, and explore each partner's emotions, needs, and defensive responses.

rows.label: Counseling Topic
rows.value: Peer relationship anxiety and school withdrawal. The client reports an automatic interpretation — 'they must dislike me' — triggered by even small changes in a peer's expression.

rows.label: Session Goals
rows.value: Examine the withdrawal that intensifies during breaks and lunchtime, and explore the recurring avoidance pattern in peer relationships alongside small steps toward connection.

rows.label: Session Summary
rows.value: A repeating cycle of peer gaze awareness → anticipated rejection → physical tension (shoulders, abdomen) → avoidance (earphones, leaving the area) was identified, with the brief relief after avoidance acting as a reinforcing factor…

rows.label: Main Intervention Direction
rows.value: Practice imagining the smallest possible approach behaviors ('hi' / 'want to walk together?') and anticipating feelings after trying them. Guide the parent toward emotion-checking-centered conversation.

rows.label: Next Session Plan
rows.value: Pick one moment in school that feels most tense, examine the physical reaction and thoughts at that time, and have the client try a short emotion-checking conversation with a parent during the week.

rows.label: Counseling Topic
rows.value: Recurring mediator role within a three-generation family and intergenerational communication conflict. The client reports emotional exhaustion and guilt from serving as an emotional translator between parents and grandparents.

rows.label: Session Goals
rows.value: Identify the mediator role the client assumes within the family, and find a first foothold for redrawn responsibility boundaries.

rows.label: Session Summary
rows.value: A three-stage communication structure — grandparents (direct expression) → client (mediation/buffer) → parents (indirect delivery) — was mapped together. A pattern in which conflict erupts directly whenever the client steps back was repeatedly confirmed…

rows.label: Main Intervention Direction
rows.value: Distinguish the belief 'I have to do this' from the client's actual area of responsibility. Explore the source of the guilt that arises when stepping back from the mediator role.

rows.label: Next Session Plan
rows.value: Differentiate things the client feels responsible for from those they do not, and record the emotions felt and the family's reactions during the week when the client withheld mediation.

05.name: Intake Interview — New client in-depth assessment

rows.label: Counseling Topic
rows.value: Initial in-depth assessment focused on recent anxiety and interpersonal stress. Accumulated stress since a role change six months ago, affecting sleep, appetite, and emotional regulation.

rows.label: Session Goals
rows.value: Organize the chief complaint and life context, and identify areas requiring follow-up in emotional regulation, interpersonal relationships, and family history.

rows.label: Session Summary
rows.value: Chief complaint was traced chronologically; difficulty with emotional regulation and recurring interpersonal conflict were examined. Sleep disruption, social isolation, and self-deprecation were identified as risk signals; clear counseling motivation and support from family and friends were noted as protective factors…

rows.label: Main Intervention Direction
rows.value: Agree on short- and medium-term counseling direction. Additional evaluation of trauma history and physical symptoms is needed; a medical referral may also be considered if warranted.

rows.label: Next Session Plan
rows.value: Differentiate short-term and long-term counseling goals, agree on the first priority area, and address trauma history and physical symptoms as additional agenda items.
